title: Ancient Egypt
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on ancient Egypt!

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"The Nile River—the longest river in the world at 4,160 miles, with floods more reliable than any other river—presented advantages available nowhere else in the world. It provided reliable transportation by boat in both directions (the current flows north, the wind blows south), making it possible for the pharaoh to control shipping and distribution in his kingdom. The river provided annual flooding, which people trapped behind dikes for its deposit of soil, then released to water plants, avoiding the evaporation that caused salinization in Sumer. Thus the Nile provided the basis for an unusual stability, while the surrounding deserts provided a natural defense."


From Cynthia Stokes Brown, _Big History: from the Big Bang to the Present_ (2007).

**Which of the following is evidence of the importance of the Nile River’s floods to Egyptian society broadly?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It allowed the pharaoh to control shipping and distribution in his kingdom
They based their seasons around the cycles of flooding
They trapped flooding behind dikes to help contain soil deposits
The floods allowed them to trade with Sumer

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The scene depicted on the macehead above is most directly the result of which of the following developments?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Image of the Scorpion macehead, dated to c. 3100 BCE. The image shows an Egyptian ruler holding a hoe and standing near a canal.
options.caption: Image of the Scorpion macehead, dated to c. 3100 BCE. The image shows an Egyptian ruler holding a hoe and standing near a canal. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scorpion_Macehead#/media/File:Scorpion_Macehead.jpg)

choices.content: Altering the environment to improve agricultural production
Controlling the environment to demonstrate the ruler’s power over nature
Creating new waterways for travel and trade
Reducing reliance on agricultural technologies

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"Hail to thee, O Nile! Who manifests thyself over this land, and comes to give life to Egypt! Mysterious is thy issuing forth from the darkness, on this day whereon it is celebrated! Watering the orchards created by Re, to cause all the cattle to live, you give the earth to drink, inexhaustible one! Path that descends from the sky, loving the bread of Seb and the first-fruits of Nepera, You cause the workshops of Ptah to prosper!

>Lord of the fish, during the inundation, no bird alights on the crops. You create the grain, you bring forth the barley, assuring perpetuity to the temples. If you cease your toil and your work, then all that exists is in anguish. If the gods suffer in heaven, then the faces of men waste away.”

From “Hymn to the Nile,” c. 2100 BCE.

**What does this passage imply was the __primary__ importance of the Nile River to Ancient Egyptian civilization?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It facilitated the spread of religion
It made the transport of monumental architecture possible
It created the opportunity for specialization of labor to develop
It made agriculture possible

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

>"As available resources increased, societies had to face, for the first time, the task of dealing with surpluses, whose control and distribution posed entirely novel problems. And, surprisingly quickly, their distribution became lopsided, so that gradients of power and wealth appeared. Surpluses began to sustain populations of privileged (and mainly male) specialists: artisans, traders, warriors, priests, scribes, and rulers.

>It is worth noting how paradoxical this steepening of hierarchies is. For the increases in productivity associated with the agrarian revolution might, in principle, have raised the average material living standards of all members of society. The reality was different. Unlike water, which prefers to lie flat as it accumulates, material wealth in complex societies likes to pile itself up into huge pyramids."


From David Christian, _Maps of Time: An Introduction to Big History_ (2004).

**What does the author argue that resource surpluses caused?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Resource surpluses caused different levels of social status to emerge
Resource surpluses raised the standard of living of all citizens, but more for some groups than others
Resource surpluses resulted in the emergence of city states
Resource surpluses gave increased power and wealth to the laborers who grew the food.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> "Now, these words, which are upon this silver tablet, are for the land of Kheta [Hittite Empire] and for the land of Egypt. As for him who shall not keep them, the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, and the thousand gods of the land of Egypt shall desolate his house, his land, and his subjects.

>Now as for him who shall keep these words, which are upon this silver tablet, whether they be of Kheta, or whether they be people of Egypt, and they shall not devise against them; the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, together with the thousand gods of the land of Egypt, shall preserve his health, and his life, together with his issue, with his land, and his subjects."

Excerpt from a peace treaty, circa 1259 BCE.

**A historian would most likely use this passage as evidence for which of the following claims:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The development of monotheistic religious beliefs
A connection of divine and political power in ancient states
The use of writing and religion to improve trade relations
Divine power was used as justification to sustain social hierarchies

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> "Now, these words, which are upon this silver tablet, are for the land of Kheta [Hittite Empire] and for the land of Egypt. As for him who shall not keep them, the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, and the thousand gods of the land of Egypt shall desolate his house, his land, and his subjects.

>Now as for him who shall keep these words, which are upon this silver tablet, whether they be of Kheta, or whether they be people of Egypt, and they shall not devise against them; the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, together with the thousand gods of the land of Egypt, shall preserve his health, and his life, together with his issue, with his land, and his subjects."

Excerpt from a peace treaty, circa 1259 BCE.


**This passage could best be understood in the context of what feature of Ancient Egyptian society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Existence of religious elites
Possession of medical knowledge
Divine kingship
Monotheistic religion

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question.

> "Now, these words, which are upon this silver tablet, are for the land of Kheta [Hittite Empire] and for the land of Egypt. As for him who shall not keep them, the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, and the thousand gods of the land of Egypt shall desolate his house, his land, and his subjects.

>Now as for him who shall keep these words, which are upon this silver tablet, whether they be of Kheta, or whether they be people of Egypt, and they shall not devise against them; the thousand gods of the land of Kheta, together with the thousand gods of the land of Egypt, shall preserve his health, and his life, together with his issue, with his land, and his subjects."

Excerpt from a peace treaty, circa 1259 BCE

**This treaty was most directly a result of which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Territorial expansion by states that were able to produce surplus resources
The codification of religious beliefs leading to a rise in religious wars
Increasing usage of precious metals, such as the widespread use of silver
Greater reliance on writing and record keeping

assessment_items.question_content:
Refer to the map below.

[[☃ image 1]]


**A historian could most persuasively use this map as evidence to argue that which of the following factors helped keep Egypt protected from potential enemies as it grew into an empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Satellite image of Nile river, acquired by NASA in 2011.
options.caption: [Satellite image of Nile river](https://www.flickr.com/photos/gsfc/5635018418), acquired by NASA in 2011.

choices.content: The Nile river currents and winds
The desert surrounding fertile river valley
The annual Nile flooding
Levees and dikes built near the river
